w: An unassuming granite boulder — a reminder that Poland cracked Enigma first. Rejewski, Różycki and Zygalski broke the German cipher in 1932; in 1939 the Cipher Bureau handed a replica machine and its methods to Britain and France, the ground Bletchley built on. The grand obelisk is in Poznań; Warsaw keeps the stone.

The Kotwica — an anchor that doubles as a P stacked over a W: Polska Walcząca, Fighting Poland. First daubed on Warsaw's walls in March 1942 from a student's design; Anna Smoleńska drew it and died in Auschwitz a year later. A four-metre steel version crowns the Warsaw Uprising Mound, heaped from the rubble of the flattened city.

One of Europe's first monuments to flight — Edward Wittig's winged airman, unveiled in 1932. The Germans tore it down in 1944; its fragments surfaced in a Wola scrapyard, and it was recast in Ochota in 1967. In 1942 Home Army scouts had painted the Fighting Poland anchor on its base — small sabotage, large nerve.

A roundabout in Ochota carries the name of the Siberian deportees — Poles driven east over centuries, above all in the Soviet deportations of 1939 to 1956. It was memory the communist state forbade: nearly every monument to them rose only after 1989. The Golgotha of the East, named at last.

An art-house cinema built into a housing estate raised, deliberately, on the levelled rubble of the Warsaw Ghetto — Bohdan Lachert's 'estate-monument' of 1951. Gutek Film has run it since 1994; its four screens carry the names of Cybulski, Pola Negri, Gérard Philipe and Ingrid Bergman. An 1866 fountain has stood out front since 1952.

An art-house cinema folded into a socialist-realist MDM apartment block, opened on a communist national holiday in July 1962. Eleonora Sekrecka designed the building, Bohdan Jezierski the interior. Two halls, a fixture of Warsaw's documentary and festival circuit on Marszałkowska — still showing the films the multiplexes won't.

Poland's oldest ethnographic museum hides behind a Venetian disguise — Henryk Marconi modelled this 1850s land-credit bank on the arcades of St Mark's Square. Inside, 81,000 objects: Polish folk costume beside collections from China, Japan and the South Pacific, and some 3,000 pieces of Carpathian Hutsul craft. Banking dressed as Venice.

Poland's largest technology collection, for decades tucked into a wing of the Palace of Culture — 15,000-odd objects, relaunched in 2022. Among them an Enigma cipher machine, Abraham Stern's early-1800s calculating engine, pre-war Sokół motorcycles and Łucznik bicycles from the Radom arms works. The machinery of a century under one roof.

Long gone now, the original opened in 1930 at Nowy Świat 67: a cabinet of criminal curiosities — counterfeiters' presses, safe-cracking kits, seized guns, and a rope ladder a prisoner had knotted from bedsheets to escape. The bombs of September 1939 destroyed it, and only photographs survive. The force keeps a quieter museum still.

Half a million rocks, fossils and dinosaur tracks, free to all — the Polish Geological Institute's museum behind a Marian Lalewicz facade of the late 1920s. A Dilophosaurus reconstruction looms inside, beside casts of footprints pressed into 206-million-year-old stone in the Holy Cross Mountains. Deep time, on Rakowiecka.
